datasets_user_files.name: ColabFold Official Databases (UniRef30, BFD/MGnify, EnvDB, PDB70/100)

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
This section contains clustered protein sequences, metagenomic sequences, environmental sequences, and curated PDB-derived sequences/structures used primarily for MSA generation and template-based structure prediction.
These datasets support Objectives 2.1, 3.1, and 4.2 by enabling MSA construction, structural context retrieval, and feature generation for protein, enzyme, and peptide foundation models, as well as Objective 5.1 for automated agentic workflows.

Aligns with the approved research topic of Bioscience.

checks.name: Prohibited Use Screening (LC 2.7)
checks.notes: No mention of sensitive terms in the documentation classified in Prohibited Use Screening in accordance with LC 2.7.

checks.name: D5+M affiliation Screening (LC 2.5)
checks.notes:
UniRef30 (2023-02)
Owner/Maintainer (Country): UniProt Consortium — United Kingdom / Switzerland / United States

BFD (Big Fantastic Database)
Owner/Maintainer (Country): Academic research consortium (MMseqs/ColabFold ecosystem) — Germany / South Korea

MGnify
Owner/Maintainer (Country): EMBL-EBI — United Kingdom

ColabFold EnvDB
Owner/Maintainer (Country): ColabFold project (Max Planck–led academic consortium) — Germany

PDB100 (FASTA)
Owner/Maintainer (Country): Worldwide Protein Data Bank (wwPDB) — United States / United Kingdom / Japan

PDB100 (FoldSeek format)
Owner/Maintainer (Country): wwPDB (data origin) with FoldSeek indexing by ColabFold/MMseqs — United States / United Kingdom / Japan (data), Germany / South Korea (indexing)

PDB70 (FASTA)
Owner/Maintainer (Country): wwPDB — United States / United Kingdom / Japan

PDB70 (mmCIF)
Owner/Maintainer (Country): wwPDB — United States / United Kingdom / Japan

No entities from D5 countries.

checks.name: Sample Inspection
checks.notes: No risk identified in LC 2.7 check. Skipping inspection.

checks.name: Provenance
checks.notes:
Ownership and maintenance are shared across UniProt Consortium, EMBL-EBI (MGnify), wwPDB, and the ColabFold project as a redistribution and indexing platform.
Source: https://colabfold.mmseqs.com/
Setup:
```
# Automated setup
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sokrypton/ColabFold/main/setup_databases.sh
chmod +x setup_databases.sh
./setup_databases.sh /path/to/database/

# Manual download
wget https://colabfold.mmseqs.com/uniref30_2302.tar.gz
wget https://colabfold.mmseqs.com/bfd_mgy_colabfold.tar.gz
wget https://colabfold.mmseqs.com/pdb70_from_mmcif_220313.tar.gz

# Extract
tar -xzf uniref30_2302.tar.gz
tar -xzf bfd_mgy_colabfold.tar.gz
tar -xzf pdb70_from_mmcif_220313.tar.gz
```

PDB-derived data: CC0 1.0 (public domain)

MGnify proteins: CC0 1.0

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
The AlphaFold3 v3.0 database contains genetic and structural reference databases for protein structure prediction, including PDB mmCIF files (~750 GB uncompressed), MGnify clusters, BFD sequences, UniRef90, UniProt, PDB SeqRes, RNACentral, NT RNA, and Rfam—totaling approximately 252 GB compressed and 630 GB uncompressed.


The AlphaFold3 database supports Thrust 2 (Foundation Models for Peptides, Enzymes, and Protein Fusions) particularly Objectives 2.1, 3.1, 4.1, and 4.2 for training Enzyme Foundation Models (EFMs), Peptide Foundation Models (PFMs), and Protein Fusion Foundation Models (PFFMs), as well as Thrust 1 objectives involving protein structure prediction and molecular dynamics simulations for biomolecular design.

Aligns with the approved research topics of Bioscience.

checks.name: Provenance
checks.notes:
Maintenance is distributed across wwPDB, UniProt Consortium, EMBL-EBI, RNAcentral Consortium, Rfam community, and NCBI, with AlphaFold3 providing standardized integration scripts.
Each component originates from its authoritative upstream scientific repository and is assembled into a unified database bundle by the AlphaFold3 project for structure inference workflows.

Official Repository: https://github.com/google-deepmind/alphafold3 Database Source: https://storage.googleapis.com/alphafold-databases/v3.0

Automated Download Script:
```
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/google-deepmind/alphafold3/main/fetch_databases.sh
chmod +x fetch_databases.sh
./fetch_databases.sh /path/to/databases/
```

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
The UniClust/UniRef30_2023_02 dataset contains clustered protein sequences from UniProtKB at 30% sequence identity with multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) in A3M format, along with mapping files linking UniProt accession codes to cluster representatives—formatted for use with HH-suite for protein homology detection.

The UniClust dataset supports Thrust 2 (Foundation Models for Peptides, Enzymes, and Protein Fusions) by providing MSAs for training Enzyme Foundation Models (EFMs), Peptide Foundation Models (PFMs), and Protein Fusion Foundation Models (PFFMs).

Aligns with the approved research topic of Bioscience.

Dr. Johannes Söding – Group Leader, Quantitative and Computational Biology, Max Planck Institute for Multidisciplinary Sciences (formerly MPI for Biophysical Chemistry), Göttingen, Germany

Dr. Martin Steinegger – Associate Professor, School of Biological Sciences, Seoul National University, South Korea

All entities are from non-D5 country.

checks.name: Provenance
checks.notes:
UniClust is produced and maintained by its original academic authors and distributed via their institutional hosting infrastructure.
The dataset is generated by clustering UniProt-derived protein sequences into families and alignments using HHsuite-compatible workflows.

Source: https://wwwuser.gwdguser.de/~compbiol/uniclust/2023_02/


Download Command:
```
# Download UniClust30 (latest 2023-02)
wget https://wwwuser.gwdguser.de/~compbiol/uniclust/2023_02/UniRef30_2023_02_hhsuite.tar.gz
tar -xzf UniRef30_2023_02_hhsuite.tar.gz

# Alternative: AWS S3 sync for unfiltered clusters
aws s3 sync --no-sign-request s3://openfold/uniclust30_unfiltered/ ./uniclust30_unfiltered_zips --only-show-errors --no-progress
```

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
OpenProteinSet contains multiple sequence alignments (MSAs) for 140,000 unique PDB chains and 16 million UniClust30 clusters, along with PDB70 template hits and AlphaFold2 structure predictions—all in A3M, HHR, and PDB formats respectively, representing over four million hours of computation.

The OpenProteinSet/OpenFold dataset supports Thrust 1 (Multi-Scale Simulation) for protein structure prediction via AlphaFold2/ColabFold pipelines, and Thrust 2 (Foundation Models) by providing precomputed MSAs for training Enzyme Foundation Models (EFMs), Peptide Foundation Models (PFMs)

Aligns with the approved research topic of Bioscience.

checks.name: Provenance
checks.notes:
The dataset is published by the OpenFold/OpenProteinSet authors and hosted via the AWS Registry of Open Data.
It is derived from standardized AlphaFold-style MSA and template generation against public protein databases.

Source: https://registry.opendata.aws/openfold/


Download Command:
```
# List available files
aws s3 ls --no-sign-request s3://openfold/

# Download specific MSA data
aws s3 sync --no-sign-request s3://openfold/msa_data/ ./openfold_msa/ --only-show-errors --no-progress

# Download template hits
aws s3 sync --no-sign-request s3://openfold/template_hits/ ./openfold_templates/ --only-show-errors --no-progress
```

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
The PDB archive contains experimentally determined 3D macromolecular structures with associated metadata.

The PDB mmCIF files dataset supports Thrust 1 (Multi-Scale Molecular Simulation) for molecular dynamics simulations and QM/MM calculations using experimentally-determined protein structures, and Thrust 2 (Foundation Models) for training protein foundation models.

Aligns with the approved research topic of Bioscience.

checks.name: Provenance
checks.notes:
The archive is owned and maintained by the Worldwide Protein Data Bank (wwPDB) consortium.
Structures are deposited by researchers worldwide, validated by wwPDB partners, and released as a public scientific archive.

Source: https://www.rcsb.org/docs/programmatic-access/file-download-services



Rsync Download Method:
```
bash
# Full PDB archive download (recommended)
rsync -rlpt -v -z --delete rsync://rsync.rcsb.org/pub/pdb/ /path/to/pdb/

# Alternative: AWS S3 sync (faster for some regions)
aws s3 sync s3://pdb-rcsb/pub/pdb/ /path/to/pdb/ --no-sign-request

#Using wget for specific structures
wget -r ftp://ftp.wwpdb.org/pub/pdb/data/structures/all/
```

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
UniRef (UniProt Reference Clusters) contains three databases of clustered protein sequences from UniProtKB and UniParc: UniRef100 (identical sequences merged), UniRef90 (90% sequence identity clusters), and UniRef50 (50% sequence identity clusters)—providing non-redundant representative sequences with functional annotations, taxonomy information, and links to source databases.

The UniRef90 dataset supports Thrust 2 (Foundation Models for Peptides, Enzymes, and Protein Fusions) by providing non-redundant protein sequence clusters for training Enzyme Foundation Models (EFMs), Peptide Foundation Models (PFMs), and Protein Fusion Foundation Models (PFFMs).

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
The mdCATH dataset contains all-atom molecular dynamics simulation trajectories for 5,398 CATH protein domains, including atomic coordinates and forces recorded every 1 ns, with 134,950 trajectories (five replicates at five temperatures from 320 K to 450 K) totaling over 62 ms of simulated time in HDF5 format with derived metadata (RMSD, RMSF, secondary structure).

The mdCATH dataset supports Thrust 1 (Multi-Scale Molecular Simulation) by providing pre-computed MD trajectories with forces for training neural network potentials and validating MD simulation protocols, as well as Thrust 2 (Foundation Models) by enabling training of data-driven machine learning potentials for protein dynamics. It aligns with the stated MD/QM/MM simulation objectives for protein conformational sampling.

Aligns with the approved research topic Bioscience.

Antonio Mirarchi – PhD Candidate, Computational Science Laboratory, UPF (Co-first author)

checks.name: Provenance
checks.notes:
Maintained by the Computational Science Laboratory research team and distributed via Hugging Face. Generated by running standardized MD simulations on CATH domain structures using validated force fields.

Source: https://huggingface.co/datasets/compsciencelab/mdCATH
Download Command:
```
bash
# Using Hugging Face CLI
huggingface-cli download compsciencelab/mdCATH --repo-type dataset --local-dir ./mdCATH/

# Or using git-lfs
git clone https://huggingface.co/datasets/compsciencelab/mdCATH
cd mdCATH
git lfs pull
```

License type: CC BY 4.0

Free to share (copy and redistribute) in any medium or format.

Free to adapt (remix, transform, and build upon) for any purpose, including commercial.

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
The database contains over 214 million AI-predicted 3D protein structures generated by AlphaFold v2.0, including model organism proteomes (46 species), global health proteomes (30 pathogens), Swiss-Prot proteins (~550,000), and the full UniProt dataset, provided in PDB and mmCIF formats with per-residue confidence scores (pLDDT) and predicted aligned error plots.

The dataset supports Thrust 2 (Foundation Models for Peptides, Enzymes, and Protein Fusions) by providing pre-computed protein structure predictions for training domain-specific foundation models, and Thrust 1 (Multi-Scale Molecular Simulation) by supplying structural templates for MD simulations and structure prediction pipelines.

Aligns with the approved research topic of Bioscience.

Database Hosting & Maintenance: EMBL-European Bioinformatics Institute (EMBL-EBI), Wellcome Genome Campus, Hinxton, UK.

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
The database contains over 214 million AI-predicted 3D protein structures generated by AlphaFold v2.0, including model organism proteomes (46 species), global health proteomes (30 pathogens), Swiss-Prot proteins (~550,000), and the full UniProt dataset, provided in PDB and mmCIF formats with per-residue confidence scores (pLDDT) and predicted aligned error plots.

The dataset supports Thrust 2 (Foundation Models for Peptides, Enzymes, and Protein Fusions) by providing pre-computed protein structure predictions for training domain-specific foundation models, and Thrust 1 (Multi-Scale Molecular Simulation) by supplying structural templates for MD simulations and structure prediction pipelines.

Aligns with the approved research topic of Bioscience.

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
The Protenix dataset is a large-scale, open-source collection of biomolecular data used to train and evaluate Protenix, a high-accuracy structure prediction model designed as an open-source reproduction of AlphaFold3. The dataset contains ~1 TB of preprocessed wwPDB training data, including raw mmCIF files (~283 GB), bioassembly structures (~36 GB), MSA files (~450 GB), CCD component files, PoseBusters benchmark data, RecentPDB structures, and sequence-to-PDB index mappings—all formatted for training AlphaFold3-style biomolecular structure prediction models.

The dataset supports Thrust 1 (Multi-Scale Molecular Simulation) by providing preprocessed structural data for protein structure prediction pipelines, and Thrust 2 (Foundation Models for Peptides, Enzymes, and Protein Fusions) as referenced in Section H of the supplementary dataset inventory ("Protenix dataset - Datasets to train protenix").

Aligns with the approved research topic of Bioscience.

checks.name: Sample Inspection
checks.notes:
Downloaded and checked the validation dataset split from https://huggingface.co/datasets/Yimingbear/protenix

The download link comes from the file https://huggingface.co/datasets/Yimingbear/protenix/blob/main/docs/training.md

Both visual and algorithmic inspection of the dataset's validation split demonstrates that the files have protein sequence data and can be visualized using rendering tools.  Notebook and HTML version of the output of the experiment is in /ibex/project/c2320/AIMCR/dataset-check/protenix . The HTML Protenix_train_data.html contains the samples inspected. Indices were extracted from the release_data.tar.gz and 100 random samples of CIF files were downloaded from https://files.rcsb.org/download/<id>.cif.gz where <id> is the index retrieved. To test that the file downloaded from RCSB database and that contained in release_data.tar.gz are the same, a diff was run on 100d.cif from RCSB and from release_data.tar.gz and both files were found to be identical. 

The samples in the Protenix_train_data.html are protein structures as mentioned in the documentation of the dataset.

checks.name: Project & Usage Alignment
checks.notes:
UniProtKB contains approximately 246 million protein sequence records comprising two sections: UniProtKB/Swiss-Prot (~570,000 manually curated, expert-reviewed entries with functional annotations) and UniProtKB/TrEMBL (automatically annotated entries from translated coding sequences), including protein/gene names, function, catalytic activity, subcellular location, post-translational modifications, sequence variants, protein-protein interactions, and cross-references to 183+ external databases.

The dataset supports Thrust 2 (Foundation Models for Peptides, Enzymes, and Protein Fusions) by providing comprehensive protein sequence databases for training and validating foundation models.

Aligns with the approved research project of Bioscience.

checks.name: Provenance
checks.notes:
Origins: Swiss-Prot created in 1986 by Amos Bairoch at University of Geneva; PIR-PSD originated from Margaret Dayhoff's Atlas of Protein Sequence and Structure (1965-1978); TrEMBL created 1996 at EBI.

UniProt Consortium formed: 2002 when EBI, SIB, and PIR merged their resources.

UniProt launched: December 2003 with NIH NHGRI funding.

Current maintenance: UniProt Consortium with updates every 8 weeks; awarded Global Core Biodata Resource status.

Download Command:
```
bash
# SwissProt
wget https://ftp.uniprot.org/pub/databases/uniprot/current_release/knowledgebase/complete/uniprot_sprot.dat.gz

# TrEMBL
wget https://ftp.uniprot.org/pub/databases/uniprot/current_release/knowledgebase/complete/uniprot_trembl.dat.gz

# UniProtKB All (FASTA format)
wget https://ftp.uniprot.org/pub/databases/uniprot/current_release/knowledgebase/complete/uniprotkb.fasta.gz

# Extract
gunzip uniprot_sprot.dat.gz
gunzip uniprot_trembl.dat.gz
gunzip uniprotkb.fasta.gz
```

Gemma-2-27B-IT is a text-to-text, decoder-only large language model designed for text generation tasks including question answering, summarization, reasoning, and conversational AI with instruction-following capabilities.

The model supports Thrust 3 (Domain-Specific Agentic AI), specifically Objective 5.2 — finetuning domain-specific LLMs to create research agents tailored to biologics design for efficient biomedical text understanding.

Aligns with the approved research topic of Datascience and Engineering.

Janus-Pro-7B is a unified multimodal large language model that performs both visual understanding (image comprehension, visual question answering, document/chart analysis) and text-to-image generation through an autoregressive framework with decoupled visual encoding pathways using SigLIP-L for understanding and a visual tokenizer for generation.

The model supports Thrust 3 (Domain-Specific Agentic AI) for multimodal tasks, listed in the proposal response document under "Multimodal Models" as "DeepSeek Janus-Pro 7B (Full Fine-tuning)" for multimodal tasks with full fine-tuning capability, potentially contributing to automated molecular design visualization and multi-scale simulation analysis workflows.

Aligns with the approved research topic of Datascience & engineering.

checks.name: Training Data Documentation
checks.notes:
The model was pretrained on multimodal data including image-text pairs, video-text pairs, and multilingual content in three stages, with the language backbone initialized from Qwen2 (pretrained on multilingual text data across 29 languages) and vision components trained on image-related data; total estimated D is 1.4 trillion multimodal tokens.

The 1.4 trillion multimodal tokens estimate for Qwen2-VL pretraining originates from the Open-Qwen2VL paper, which states that its own 5B token training is 0.36% of Qwen2-VL's 1.4T multimodal pretraining tokens, and a Medium article detailing Qwen2-VL's two-stage process with 600B initial tokens plus 800B image-related tokens totaling 1.4T.

checks.name: Sample Inspection
checks.notes: No risk identified in LC 2.7 and low risk in LC 2.5 check. Skipping inspection.

observations:
- The software list is very small. 

- Protenix Dataset has D5 country affiliation. The dataset is a large-scale, open-source collection of biomolecular data used to train and evaluate Protenix, a high-accuracy structure prediction model designed as an open-source reproduction of AlphaFold3. The risk is reduced to low after the inspection, which shows that the samples are all protein sequences, and can be downloaded and visualized using Python tools. 

- URL for EZSpecificity (a database containing over 214 million AI-predicted 3D protein structures generated by AlphaFold v2.0) is a path on Ibex, but can also be downloaded from the internet after registration. 

- Qwen2-VL-72B-Instruct, Janus-Pro-7B, DeepSeek-R1, DeepSeek-V3, and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-70B have D5 country affiliations. The risk is reduced to low because of their provenance and alignment with the proposed use in the project, given the generalization and post-training specializations of the tasks.
recommendation:
The software list is a bit short. There is a possibility that the PI may request more software after the first experiments on  Shaheen III GPUP. 

The Protenix dataset is set to low risk because the findings in sample inspection align with the description of the dataset, and the files contain protein sequences.   

The PI has requested 4 models developed and maintained in the D5 country. Some are text-to-text, and others are multimodal with visual and text capabilities. The risk has been lowered to low because they are well-recognized and popular models. Also, the intent to use these models for fine-tuning using protein datasets for biologics tasks, along with visual input of protein structure, aligns with the documented capabilities of the model architectures. All models all pass the LC 2.7 check. Through the email response in the ticketing system, the Co-PI has confirmed that they are well aware of the sensitivity and did not​ and will not​ collaborate with any of ByteDance, Deepseek or Alibaba.

As an additional precaution, monitoring of the use of the models is proposed midway through the project to ensure that only the proposed datasets are used to train the models from D5 entities. This check will ensure no new dataset(s) have been introduced in the training process that deviates from the proposed plan in the project proposal.

Positive check pass.

Negative check pass. 

10^27 check pass. 

Recommended to proceed with the grand challenge.
